coredata.dc:title: Fe, N-doped carbon dots/RF in self-Fenton cascade reaction: Improved photodegradation mechanism and toxicity evaluation

coredata.dc:description: The nanozyme, identified among the top ten emerging technologies in chemistry, has shown rapid development in nano-catalysis for tumour treatment, particularly through its Fenton-like reaction. Herein, a novel iron, nitrogen-doped carbon dots (Fe, N-CDs) with nanozyme catalytic properties was designed, which was then surface-modified onto resorcinol-formaldehyde (RF) resin. Subsequently, a heterogeneous photo-Fenton-like cascade system was constructed to produce and activate photo-generated hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) in situ. Within a broad pH range of 5–9, Fe, N-CDs/RF composites demonstrated superior photocatalytic activity compared to RF alone. Under visible light irradiation, degradation of chloroquine phosphate (CQ) was achieved with a degradation rate constant (k) 3.2 times higher than that observed for RF. Active species capture experiments revealed that hydroxyl radicals (•OH) and superoxide radicals (•O2 -) are crucial in propelling the photocatalytic reaction. Furthermore, Density Functional Theory (DFT) calculations indicated that introduction of nanozymes enhances the transfer of electrons from RF surfaces to Fe, N-CDs, high adsorption ability of Fe, N-CDs towards H2O2 (Eads=-5.45 eV) in the Fe, N-CDs/RF composites was exploited, thereby augmenting their photocatalytic activity. The possible degradation mechanism of CQ was proposed, and environmental toxicity of CQ degradation intermediates was assessed by seed experiments. This study extends the application scope of carbon dot nanozymes in self-sufficient photo-Fenton systems.

XRD (a) and ESR spectra (b) of the as-prepared catalysts.
Survey XPS spectrum of (a) RF, (b) Fe, N-CDs/RF-0.3 %, high-resolution (c) Fe 2p, (d) C 1 s, (e) N 1 s, and (f) O 1 s.
(a) UV–vis DRS, Eg plots (inset), (b) Band structure alignments.

(b) The pseudo-first-order reaction kinetics of all photocatalysts. (c-d) Different water quality on the degradation performance of photocatalysts for CQ (20 mg/L). (e) Cycling tests for CQ degradation initiated by Fe, N-CDs/RF-0.3 % as the photocatalyst. (f) The degradation rate of Fe, N-CDs/RF-0.3 % on CQ for cycling every 90 min, Data were plotted as mean of three replicates with error bars indicating relative deviation from the mean.

Left: Degradation performance of different pollutants solutions [RhB, CR]=50 mg/L, [TC, p-NP]=10 mg/L, [MB] = 5 mg L–1. Reaction conditions: catalyst = 10 mg. Right: the average rate constants (k) of different pollutants.

(a) PL spectra, (b) Electrochemical impedance maps, (c) Time-resolved fluorescence transient spectra, (d) Transient photocurrent response.
(a) Free radical capture experiment. EPR spectra of (b) DMPO-OH•, (c) DMPO-O2 −•, and (d) TEMP-1O2.

(d) Calculated charge density difference, where the blue indicated charge loss and yellow regions indicated charge accumulation. ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The nanozyme, identified among the top ten emerging technologies in chemistry, has shown rapid development in nano-catalysis for tumour treatment, particularly through its Fenton-like reaction. Herein, a novel iron, nitrogen-doped carbon dots (Fe, N-CDs) with nanozyme catalytic properties was designed, which was then surface-modified onto resorcinol-formaldehyde (RF) resin. Subsequently, a heterogeneous photo-Fenton-like cascade system was constructed to produce and activate photo-generated hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) in situ. Within a broad pH range of 5–9, Fe, N-CDs/RF composites demonstrated superior photocatalytic activity compared to RF alone. Under visible light irradiation, degradation of chloroquine phosphate (CQ) was achieved with a degradation rate constant (k) 3.2 times higher than that observed for RF. Active species capture experiments revealed that hydroxyl radicals (•OH) and superoxide radicals (•O2 -) are crucial in propelling the photocatalytic reaction. Furthermore, Density Functional Theory (DFT) calculations indicated that introduction of nanozymes enhances the transfer of electrons from RF surfaces to Fe, N-CDs, high adsorption ability of Fe, N-CDs towards H2O2 (Eads=-5.45 eV) in the Fe, N-CDs/RF composites was exploited, thereby augmenting their photocatalytic activity. The possible degradation mechanism of CQ was proposed, and environmental toxicity of CQ degradation intermediates was assessed by seed experiments. This study extends the application scope of carbon dot nanozymes in self-sufficient photo-Fenton systems.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Chloroquine phosphate (CQ) is used as a generic drug for the treatment and prevention of malaria, amoebiasis, and rheumatoid arthritis. In 2020, CQ is receiving increasing attention as a possible treatment for coronavirus disease [1]. Notably, the persistent and bio-accumulative nature of CQ toxicity may pose a potential threat to organisms and the environment. Commonly used removal methods include adsorption, electrocatalytic oxidation [2], sulfate advanced oxidation process (SR-AOP) [3], and Fenton oxidation techniques [4,5].

The Fenton and Fenton-like reactions find extensive use in treating refractory wastewater [6] and tumor catalytic therapy [7], owing to their potent oxidation capability and environmentally friendly nature. In a homogeneous solution, Fe2+ serves as an activator for H2O2, undergoing conversion into hydroxyl radical (•OH) with an oxidation potential of +2.8 V, capable of degrading most organic pollutants and eradicating bacteria. However, this reaction requires acidic conditions (pH=3–4), and continuous addition of H2O2, and leads to the generation of a significant amount of iron sludge [8]. The emergence of the photocatalysis-self-Fenton system (PSFs), which operates without the need for additional H2O2 and involves the accelerated circulation of Fe2+/Fe3+, has garnered considerable attention [9,10]. However, the two technical challenges persist in the PSFs: the sustainable in situ generation of sufficient H2O2 to initiate the Fenton reaction and the effective activation of H2O2 for •OH production [4]. Consequently, numerous scientists have devoted significant efforts to selecting and structurally regulating photocatalysts.

The photochemical conversion of O2 to H2O2 has emerged as a promising approach for H2O2 production [11]. In particular, organic photocatalysts like, g-C3N4 [12], perylene diimide (PDI) [13], and porphyrin [14] have made great breakthroughs in the photocatalytic production of H2O2, H2, and O2, but they still encountered challenges due to limited visible light absorption, low activity, and reliance on sacrificial reagents. Shiraishi et al. [15] demonstrated that elevating the synthesis temperature of resorcinol-formaldehyde (RF) resin, a metal-free photocatalyst with a narrow band gap, significantly enhanced its capability to generate H2O2 (H2O+0.5 O2→H2O2, ΔG = 117 kJ/mol), without the need for sacrificial reagents, attributed to the benzenoid and quinoid electron donor-acceptor (D-A) coupling [16]. Subsequently, researchers further enhanced its H2O2 evolution by incorporating polythiophene [17], benzoxazine [18], rGO [19], and graphene quantum dots [20].

In activating H2O2 and converting it into •OH, researchers have employed iron-based materials [21,22] such as FeOOH [23], FeS2 [24],and ferric-ellagate [25] to construct heterogeneous photo-Fenton systems with RF, showcasing remarkable applications in organic degradation. Initially proposed by Gao et al. [26], magnetite nanoparticles (Fe3O4 NPs) was found to exhibit catalytic behavior similar to horseradish peroxidase (HRP). These nanoparticles offer advantages such as low toxicity and economic feasibility, rendering them ideal catalysts for dye removal in Fenton-like processes. However, the problem of partial dissolution and iron loss in solution of Fe3O4 NPs requires attention [27]. As zero-dimensional carbon-based nanomaterials, carbon dots (CDs) have attracted widespread attention in photocatalysis H2 production and pollutant degradation due to their excellent electron transfer characteristics (sp2) [28,29], unique upconversion photoluminescence [30,31], abundant oxygen-containing functional groups, and controllable structural characteristics [32]. CDs have been combined with BiOX [33], BiOCl [34], FeOCl [35], ZnO [36], TiO2 [37,38] to promote interface charge transfer and suppress electron-hole recombination [39,40], thhereby enhancing the degradation efficiency towards organic pollutants [41,42]. Furthermore, metal ions can establish coordination bonds with oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of CDs, enhancing the stability of metal materials while demonstrating excellent catalytic activity and selectivity [43]. Li et al. [28] synthesised a FeN-CDs/TiO2@CN multi-heterojunction photocatalyst with good photocatalytic nitrogen-fixing activity. Lu et al. [8] designed a homogeneous cobalt-doped carbon-dot nanozyme for effective tumor therapy by boosting reactive oxygen species production. Zhang et al. [44] reported a novel CD-COOFe(III) photocatalyst which significantly enhanced the rate of •OH generation by facilitating rapid electron and proton transfer [30]. Pan et al. [45] effectively eliminated resilient bacterial biofilms using chitosan-grafted iron-doped carbon dots (CS@Fe/CDs) nanozymes. Nano-enzyme chemistry, recognized as one of the top ten emerging technologies, has found successfully applications in chemical sensing, biomedicine, and other domains.

Based on this, a nanozyme-photocatalytic cascade reaction system was proposed for the efficient production and activation of H2O2. In this study, Fe, N-CDs with nanozyme catalytic properties were synthesized successfully via the hydrothermal method and then combined with RF using EDC-NHS coupling to establish an in situ cascade photocatalytic degradation system ( Fig. 1). This effectively addresses the challenge of achieving high efficiency in H2O2 production and activation. The rapid transfer and separation photo-electrons generated by RF can be attributed to the formation of an interface between Fe, N-CDs and RF. Additionally, the narrowed band gap and negatively shifted conduction band contribute to the enhanced generation of H2O2. DFT calculations confirmed that Fe, N-CDs as active site demonstrated a strong adsorption ability for H2O2 (Eads=-5.45 eV), facilitating its decomposition into •OH and resulting in a higher CQ degradation rate (3.2 times that of RF). This study proposes a cascade reaction system that combines nanozyme catalysis with the photocatalysis of RF, providing insights for developing similar photocatalysis-self-Fenton stage to address organic pollutants.

The chemicals like β-cyclodextrin (β-CD), resorcinol, phenol, formaldehyde (HCHO), oxalic acid, terephthalic acid (TPA), and triethylenetetramine (TETA) were procured from Macklin Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. Deionised water was prepared in the laboratory. CQ, methylene blue (MB), rhodamine B (RhB), congo red (CR), tetracycline (TC), and p-nitrophenol (p-NP) obtained from Tianjin Kemiou Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. Potassiumiodide (KI), isopropanol (IPA) and p-benzoquinone (PBQ), citric acid (CA), FeSO4.7 H2O were supplied by Macklin Biochemical Technology Co. Ltd. All chemicals were used as received without any further purification.

RF was prepared using a one-step hydrothermal method following established procedure [46]. Specifically, 0.05 g phenol, 0.56 g formaldehyde, 0.35 g resorcinol and 0.54 mL 1 mol L−1 Oxalic acid were added to 40 mL deionised water and subjected to stirring at 25±2 ℃ for 24 h. The resulting suspension was then transferred to a 200 mL Teflon autoclave and reacted at 250°C for 12 h. The resulting red solid was obtained by filtration through a 0.1 μm filter membrane and subsequently washed thrice with deionised water and ethanol, respectively. Finally, the obtained RF was dried in a vacuum oven at 60 ℃ for 24 h.

The Fe, N-CDs were prepared using a one-pot hydrothermal method [47]. In summary, 1.2 g of β-CD, 1.2 g of CA, 3.12 g of FeSO4·7 H2O, and 2.4 mL of TETA were added to 120 mL of deionized water. After magnetic stirring for 1 h, the mixture was transferred to a 200 mL Teflon autoclave and reacted at 250 °C for 5 h. After cooling to room temperature, the photocatalyst was obtained by centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for 15 min, and the supernatant was filtered through a 0.22 μm filter membrane to eliminate insoluble materials. The solution was subsequently dialysed using a 3500 Da for 24 h to remove the residual small molecules, with deionised water replaced every 6 h. The purified Fe, N-CDs were then freeze-dried and stored in a dark environment. The synthetic yield of purified CDs was 13.0±2.1 %.

Fe, N-CDs and RF were compounded using the EDC-NHS coupling method. The procedure was as follows: 0.1 g of RF was dispersed in 25 mL of dopamine solution (2 g/L) with ultrasound assistance and filtered after stirring for 30 min. Subsequently, 25 mL of Fe, N-CDs activated by EDC/NHS were added and stirred in the dark at 500 rpm for 24 h. The precipitate was collected by passing it through a 0.1 μm filter membrane, followed by washing it with deionized water and ethanol. Subsequently, it was dried under vacuum at 60 °C for 24 h. The resulting samples were denoted as Fe, N-CDs/RF-x (x = 0.1, 0.3, 0.5, 1 %), where x represents the mass fraction of Fe, N-CDs in the composites. Similarly, the N-CDs/RF was prepared using the same procedure, with N-CDs replacing Fe, N-CDs. To ensure reproducibility of performance, repeated synthesis was performed in triplicate (n = 3) according to the synthesis method for CDs, RF, and CDs/RF.

The morphology and elemental composition of the samples underwent characterization using a ZEISS Gemini 300 scanning electron microscope (SEM) equipped with an EDS mapping module and FEI Tecnai G2 F20 high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM). Furthermore, their elemental composition, chemical valence state, and electronic structure information of the samples were analysed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (Thermo Scientific K-Alpha) using AL Kα-rays as the excitation source. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were obtained using an FLS1000 spectrometer with an excitation wavelength of 365 nm. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded using a Nicolet 6700 spectrophotometer (Thermo-fisher). UV-Vis NIR Diffuse Reflectance infrared absorption diagrams of samples were acquired using a Shimadzu UV-2600/2700 spectrometer. BaSO4 served as a reference, and the test range was 200 nm–800 nm. The electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) signals of free radicals were analysed using an EMXPLUS spectrometer (Bruker) under visible light irradiation, with DMPO serving as the free radical trapping agent. Electrochemical and photoelectrochemical measurements were performed on a Zahner Zennium XC workstation and a three-electrode system. The catalyst-modified ITO as the working electrode, a platinum electrode served as the counter electrode, and Ag/AgCl were utilised as the reference electrode. The electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) of the samples were annlysed using the AC impedance mode of the electrochemical workstation (Zennium XC) in the frequency range of 0.1 Hz–100 kHz, employing an AC voltage amplitude of 10 mV. Instantaneous photocurrent tests were conducted utilising the Chrono Amperometry mode (For detail of analytical methodologies, refer to the supporting information).

A 300 W xenon lamp (PLS-SXE300, Perfect Light Technology, Co. Ltd, China, 35.2 mW/cm2) served as the visible light source, employing a 420 nm cut-off filter. The distance between the light source and the top of the sample solution was set at 8 cm. The reaction temperature was controlled by circulating water through the outer layer of the reactor. CQ was selected as the organic pollutant of interest. The adsorption capacity of the catalysts was assessed by measuring the absorbance of CQ solution. For this purpose, 5 mg, 10 mg, 20 mg, and 40 mg of catalyst were individually dispersed in the 50 mL of 20 mg L−1 CQ solution. Subsequently, the mixtures were stirred continuously in darkness for 30 min to achieve adsorption equilibrium. Following this duration, 5 mL of the mixture was extracted, filtered using a needle filter with a pore size of 0.22 μm, and subjected to measurement by high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) (Thermo Scientific UltiMate™ 3000) at 342 nm, which was equipped with a UV–vis detector and a C18 reversed-phase column (4.6 mm × 250 mm, 5 μm). Acetonitrile and 0.3 % triethylamine (10:90, v/v) were used as mobile phase with the flow rate of 1.0 mL min−1 and a column temperature of 25℃. The intermediates of degradation were analyzed by LC–MS (Agilent 1290II-6460、AB5600).To investigate the influence of pH value on photocatalytic degradation, the pH of the mixture solution was adjusted by adding either 0.1 M NaOH or HCl solution.

Precisely, 10 mg of catalyst was individually dispersed in 50 mL of CQ solution and water under magnetic stirring. The mixture was stirred in the dark for 30 min. Subsequently, the power supply for the photocatalytic reaction was activated to initiate H2O2 production. Approximately 5 mL of liquid was withdrawn from the mixture every 30 min, and the concentration of H2O2 in the solution was assessed post-centrifugation to eliminate the catalyst as outlined in previous studies [48].

The qualitative measurement of generated •OH radicals was performed by high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [49]. Further details of the method can be referenced in the supplementary information.
The detailed parameters are available in the supporting information.

The toxicity of CQ and metabolites were evaluated by Toxicity Estimation Software (T.E.S.T.) and seed germination experiments. Rye seeds, Sudan grass seeds tall fescue seeds of uniform size were immersed in 2 % H2O2 for 5 min, washed repeatedly with deionized water, and 50 seeds were selected and placed in a 90 mm Petri dish containing 2 equal-sized filter papers with 10 mL deionized water, CQ and degraded solution of CQ at 60 min and 120 min, respectively. The dishes were placed in an incubator at a constant temperature of 25°C for 3 days, the germination rate was calculated, and incubation was continued for another 7 days, and the lengths of the roots and trails were measured. The cultivate solution was kept at 10 mL during incubation by adding aforementioned solution.

The morphology of Fe, N-CDs/RF was characterised using SEM and TEM. SEM analysis revealed that the RF resin comprised spherical particles with diameters ranging from 2 to 5 μm ( Fig. 2a), consistent with findings reported in the literature [46]. TEM images showed demonstrated the successful synthesis of Fe, N-CDs (Fig. 2c), displaying water solubility, good dispersion, and an average diameter of approximately 4.17 nm (Fig. 2d). The lattice spacing of the Fe, N-CDs was measured at 0.203 nm, closely matching the facet of graphitic carbon (inset of Fig. 2c) [50]. Upon composite with Fe, N-CDs, a distinct layer was observed on the surface of RF (Fig. 2b), which corresponded with the TEM images in Fig. 2e and f. Additionally, EDS mapping studies elucidated the distribution of Fe, N-CDs on the RF surface (Fig. 2g–j). The blue fluorescence is exhibited by Fe, N-CDs and N-CDs when irradiated with UV light at 365 nm, (Fig. S1). To investigate the interaction between RF and Fe, N-CDs, zeta potential measurement were conducted. The surface zeta potential of Fe, N-CDs was determined to be positive (8.12 ± 0.62 mV), whereas that of N-CDs was negative (-6.26±0.4 mV) (Fig. S2). These results confirm the successful doping of Fe into CDs to produce Fe, N-CDs.

The crystal structures of RF, Fe, N-CDs, and Fe, N-CDs/RF with varying Fe, N-CDs contents were examined using XRD. As depicted in Fig. 3a, a broad diffraction peak was observed at 2θ = 22.7°, corresponding to the (002) plane of graphitic carbon (ICSD No. 617290), indicating the π-stacked conjugated aromatic structure of RF and Fe, N-CDs [24,46]. In addition, some peaks of Fe3C (JCPDS No. 35–0772)[51] were observed in Fe, N-CDs, but not observed in catalysts with different Fe, N-CDs content, possibly because Fe was too little to be detected. Upon modification with Fe, N-CDs, no new peaks emerged, likely owing to the structural similarity between RF and CDs. However, as the loading amount Fe, N-CDs increased, the peak intensity weaken, this can be attributed to the disordered accumulation of CDs.

The spin states of unpaired electrons in the photocatalysts were analysed using electron spin resonance spectroscopy (ESR). The Lorentz lines (g = 2.005) revealed that the intensity of Fe, N-CDs/RF-0.3 % was notably higher compared to that of RF, N-CDs/RF-0.3 %, Fe, N-CDs/RF-0.1 %, Fe, N-CDs/RF-0.3 %, Fe, N-CDs/RF-0.5 % and Fe, N-CDs/RF-1.0 % (Fig. 3b). This observation suggests that the addition of 0.3 % Fe, N-CDs to the RF resin led to an increase in the number of unpaired electrons, thereby

enhancing electron delocalization [16]. However, with the increase of Fe, N-CDs loading amount, the number of unpaired electrons decreased. It is reasonable to speculate that an excessive quantity of carbon nanodots react with RF to form chemical bonds, resulting in a reduction in the number of unpaired electrons.

The chemical structure and functional groups of the catalysts were assessed using FT-IR spectroscopy (Fig. S3). The peaks observed in the range of 3000–3500 cm−1 for all samples were attributed to O-H or N-H vibration, and these peaks at 2925 and 2964 cm−1 peaks intensified with increasing Fe, N-CDs content, indicating the presence of alkyl chains such as -CH2 and -CH3 in the Fe, N-CDs. Moreover, prominent absorption bands in the range of 1200–1650 cm−1, corresponding to the stretching vibration of aromatic carbon-nitrogen heterocycles, with the two major absorption peaks at 1600 and 1450 cm−1 corresponding to the vibration of benzene rings. The presence of C-O-C stretching vibration at 1090 cm−1 suggested that the composite catalyst exhibited good water solubility [52]. However, the successful combination of RF and Fe, N-CDs could not be conclusively analysed from FT-IR spectra due to the similarity of functional groups between RF and Fe, N-CDs contain similar functional groups.

To further investigate the composition and surface properties of the photocatalyst, a representative sample of Fe, N-CDs/RF-0.3 % was selected for XPS analysis, as depicted in Fig. 4. The full XPS spectrum of Fe, N-CDs, Fe, N-CDs/RF demonstrated the presence of C, N, O, and Fe elements (Figs. 4a, S4a), whereas no Fe element was detected in RF, indicating the successful loading of Fe, N-CDs onto the surface of RF (Fig. 4b). Further examination of the Fe 2p spectrum revealed characteristic peaks at ∼709.2 eV and ∼722 eV, corresponding to Fe 2p3/2 and Fe 2p1/2 states, respectively (Figs. 4c, S4b). Furthermore, we observed that Fe (II) predominated in the composite material, with an area greater (59.9 %) than that of Fe (III), indicating that iron primarily existed in the form of Fe (II) [45,53] (Table S1). High-resolution C1s XPS analysis of Fe, N-CDs/RF composites revealed characteristic peaks at 284.8 eV, 286.07 eV, and 287.3 eV, attributable to C-C, C-O and CO bonds in RF and Fe, N-CDs, respectively (Figs. 4d, S4c). The C-OH and CO functional groups contributed by CDs were located near 286.3 and 286.0 eV [52]. With the incorporation of Fe, N-CDs, the area of the CO peak increased from 2.6 % to 31.1 %, which may be attributed to the presence of oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of CDs [54] (Table S2). Additionally, while the XPS of N 1 s in RF exhibited no nitrogen signal, three distinct peaks at 399.12 eV, 400.3 eV were observed in Fe, N-CDs/RF, corresponding to the C-N-C (hybridized N), N-(C)3 and N-H bonds of Fe, N-CDs, respectively (Fig. S4d) [45]. Regarding the XPS of O 1 s, in addition to the C-O peak at 532.86 eV in RF, a new peak appeared at 531.17 eV, also observed in Fe, N-CDs (Fig. S4e). This observation suggests the potential formation of C-Fe-O bonds, consistent with previous reports [55].

To investigate whether the incorporation of Fe, N-CDs improved visible-light absorption, UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (UV–vis DRS) was conducted. As illustrated in Fig. 5a, RF exhibited noticeable absorption characteristics in the visible region, stemming from the charge transfer transition between D-A ligands [56]. With the modification of N-CDs, the absorption intensity of photocatalyst in the visible region was augmented. Moreover, the combination of Fe, N-CDs and RF resulted in a substantial enhancement in the intensity and rang of visible-light absorption. This enhancement could be ascribed to the synergistic effect of improved light absorption capacity and electron delocalization degree of RF in the presence of Fe, N-CDs.

The observations were further validated by Tauc plot analysis (inset image in Fig. 5a). Specifically, the estimated band gap (Eg) for RF, N-CDs/RF, and Fe, N-CDs/RF were found to be 1.92 eV, 1.87 eV, and 1.66 eV, respectively. The narrowing of band gap could be attributed to the presence of a PDA layer between RF and Fe, N-CDs, which facilitated charge transfer from RF to Fe, N-CDs and introduced additional p states in the band structure.

To further elucidate the band structures of the photocatalysts, the flat band potential of the composite catalysts was investigated using the Mott-Schottky curve (Fig. S5). Relative to the reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE), RF, N-CDs/RF and Fe, N-CDs/RF exhibited flat band positions at 0.38 V, 0.23 V and 0.08 V respectively. The positive slope indicated n-type semiconductor behavior, where the flat band potential (EFB) is approximately equal to the conduction band potential (ECB) [57]. Integrating Eg calculated from DSR, it can be inferred that the conduction band position of Fe, N-CDs/RF was 0.08 eV, while ECB of RF was 0.38 eV, suggesting a higher reductive capacity for O2 to H2O2 evolution with more negative ECB positions [58]. Combing with the equation (EVB = ECB + Eg), the valence band edge positions of the catalysts were finally determined (Fig. 5b). In all cases, their EVB and ECB exhibited the sufficient capacity to oxidise water (+1.23 V RHE) and reduce O2 (+0.68 V RHE) to produce H2O2. Notably, the ECB of Fe, N-CDs/RF (+0.08 V) was more negative than that of RF (+0.38 V) and N-CDs/RF (+0.23 V), accounting for the higher reducing ability of O2 and the increased production of H2O2 observed in Fe, N-CDs/RF [59].

To ascertain the catalytic ability of Fe, N-CDs in generating ROS through H2O2, a standard experimental assay for peroxidase-like activity was conducted. In this assay, compounds with peroxidase-like activity will oxidise TMB to yield a blue color in the presence of H2O2. As shown in Fig. S6a, a distinct absorption peak at 652 nm was observed only when Fe, N-CDs, TMB, and H2O2 coexisted, accompanied by a blue solution in the UV–visible absorption spectrum. Conversely, no blue coloration occurred when only TMB and H2O2 were present, or when only Fe, N-CDs and TMB coexisted. This observation indicates that Fe, N-CDs lack inherent oxidation capability. For comparison, N-CDs was synthesized using the same method did not exhibit nanozyme characteristics similar to those of Fe, N-CDs, with no absorption peak detected at 652 nm (Fig. S6b). Therefore, the presence of iron is inferred to play a pivotal role in determining the catalytic activity of Fe, N-CDs.

We hypothesised that the incorporation of nanozyme significantly influenced the degradation efficiency of RF in the self-Fenton reaction under visible light. Thus, we studied the photocatalytic activity of Fe, N-CDs/RF photocatalysts with varying proportions of Fe, N-CDs using CQ as a model pollutant. Initially, we examined the photo-induced degradation of CQ without catalyst ( Fig. 6a). After one hour, CQ exhibited a partial dissolution rate of approximately 2 % (Blank in Fig. 6a) in the absence of catalyst. While with 10 mg RF photocatalysts, CQ degradation rate was only 63.3 %, possibly due to electron-hole recombination. With N-CDs loaded onto RF catalysts, CQ degradation efficiency reached 71.9 %. Remarkably, in the Fe, N-CDs decorated RF system, degradation efficiency reached 95.8 %, indicating a crucial role of Fe in catalytic degradation by serving as an active site for photo-induced electron transfer and separation. The sample doped with 0.3 % Fe, N-CDs exhibited superior photocatalytic performance, achieving a remarkable CQ degradation rate of 95.8 % and a pseudo-first-order rate constant (k) of 0.0206 min−1. These values were significantly higher compared to those achieved by RF or N-CDs/RF systems alone, with factors of approximately 3.2 and 2.5 times, respectively (Fig. 6b). Excessive amounts of CDs may lead to self-aggregation, hindering the efficient generation of photoelectrons via RF catalyst [60], thus affecting CQ degradation performance.

Reaction conditions significantly influence the efficiency of catalytic reactions, with catalyst dosage and solution pH playing crucial roles. As depicted in Fig. S7a, the adsorption of CQ was improved with increasing the catalyst dosage from 5 mg to 40 mg under dark conditions. However, equilibrium was reached with a noticeable enhancement in catalytic efficiency when using a 10 mg catalyst under identical conditions. Excessive catalyst amount could lead to agglomeration and increased light scattering [34,61]. The pH of pollutant solutions is another significant factor to consider in the photo-Fenton system. As shown in Fig. S7b, the degradation rate of CQ initially increased and then decreased in response to pH variations. Specifically, as the pH increased from 3 to 5, the degradation rate of CQ gradually rose and subsequently declined. To investigate the underlying mechanism governing the influence of pH on CQ degradation, we measured the pKa values and H2O2 residual content for Fe, N-CDs/RF. The obtained pKa value for Fe, N-CDs/RF was approximately 4.5, further confirming the regulatory role of protons from carboxylate groups present on Fe, N-CDs surfaces [28]. Low (＜3) or high (＞12) pH levels were found to inhibit the production and utilisation of H2O2 (Fig. S8), although lower pH values can enhance the oxidising capacity of •OH [62]. Moreover, these extreme pH conditions may compromise the integrity of the polymer catalyst surface structure and lead to the dissolution of iron ions from Fe, N-CDs. The results illustrate that Fe, N-CDs/RF could compensate for the lack of stringent pH requirements of the traditional Fenton system and enable the photo-Fenton reaction to produce active species in the neutral range.

Despite selecting an initial pH of 5.0 to minimize cost and complexity, significant degradation effects on CQ persisted. Despite this, the system still exhibited significant degradation effects on CQ. Thus, we chose to employ 10 mg Fe, N-CDs/RF-0.3 % for pollutant degradation experiments under visible light.

In addition, in order to further investigate the effect of different water qualities on the degradation of CQ by the Fe, N-CDs/RF, lake water, tap water and deionized water were used as the background for the degradation of CQ experiments, respectively (Fig. 6c-d). From the experiments, it can be seen that in tap water, the photocatalyst showed a higher CQ degradation activity, indicating that the ions in water have less influence on the catalytic degradation activity of the catalyst for CQ degradation, and it can be widely applied to different water qualities, which is of some value in practical applications. Meanwhile, stability testing on Fe, N-CDs/RF-0.3 % (Fig. 6d) showed a slight decline in catalytic activity after five cycles, remaining above 85 % (Fig. 6f). However, it offered the advantage of easy removal by gravity due to precipitation after 24 hours of reaction (Fig. S9), avoiding issues associated with powder triggers.

Fe content in Fe, N-CDs was quantified using ICP–MS analysis, showing a mass concentration of merely 2.3 %. After five cycles of recycling, no Fe was detected. This suggesting the intact structural integrity of the photocatalyst.

To evaluate the applicability of Fe, N-CDs/RF-0.3 %, the degrading efficiency of various organic pollutants including rhodamine B (RhB), Congo red (CR), tetracycline (TC), p-nitrophenol (p-NP), and methylene blue (MB) were measured, as shown in Fig. 7. The catalyst exhibited a high efficiency in degrading MB, and lowest for p-NP. As shown in Fig. S10, the degradation process of pollutants above-mentioned followed a pseudo-first order kinetics, and the average rate constants (k) of RhB, CR, TC, p-NP, and MB were 0.02022, 0.01132, 0.01258, 0.00492, and 0.0355 min−1, respectively (Fig. 7). The results showed that Fe, N-CDs/RF-0.3 % had good versatility in the removal of pollutants.

The improved degradation of dyes and antibiotics by Fe, N-CDs/RF in the self-Fenton photocatalytic system was attributed to the efficient H2O2 production by RF and the efficient activation of H2O2 by Fe, N-CDs. Compared with the degradation performance of the target pollutants among previously reported (summarized in Table S3), the advanced oxidation system based on peroxymonosulfate (PMS) exhibits the highest degradation efficiency for CQ, the next is electrocatalytic oxidation system, but it has the problems of high energy consumption and difficulty in achieving industrial application. Compared with the catalysts used in visible-light photocatalysis reported in the literatures, Fe, N-CDs/RF composites in this study exhibited high degradation performance toward CQ, TC, and RhB, while the performance to degrade p-NP and MB remains to be improved.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In situ generation and activation of H2O2
ce:section.ce:para: To further investigate the photocatalytic degradation process of Fe, N-CDs/RF on CQ, we assessed in situ generation and utilization of H2O2 in the sample. Fig. S11 depicts the amount of H2O2 produced by Fe, N-CDs/RF-x. For clearly comparison of their catalytic activities, the rate of H2O2 evolution by the photocatalysts was calculated under visible light for 1 hour ( Fig. 8a). The results indicate that RF alone exhibited poor H2O2 production activity. Incorporating N-CDs on the surface of RF slightly increased the catalytic activity, though not significantly. However, when Fe, N-CDs were combined with RF, the evolution rate of H2O2 significantly increased. Particularly, at a Fe, N-CDs content of 0.3 %, the photocatalytic activity peaked at 847 μmol g−1 h−1, 1.5 times higher than that of RF. However, excessive Fe, N-CDs content led to decreased catalytic activity. This could be attributed to the excessive CDs increasing light absorption intensity and suppressing catalyst electron excitation [57]. Under identical experimental conditions, Fe, N-CDs alone exhibited negligible H2O2 production, which remained consistent regardless of increased irradiation time. To further explore the photocatalytic decomposition ability of Fe, N-CDs, residual H2O2 levels were analysed (Fig. S12). The rate of self-degraded H2O2 was calculated to be 23 % after 1 hour of photocatalytic reaction. The decomposition rate of H2O2 in the presence of Fe, N-CDs reached 80 %, significantly higher than when using N-CDs alone. This indicates that RF is primarily responsible for H2O2 production, while Fe, N-CDs promote the in situ decomposition of H2O2 (consistent with the results in Fig. S6) and accelerate the cycle of Fe3+ and Fe2+ via photo-induced charge separation. Compared with other catalysts such as rGO/RF [57], RF-CN [63], RF/ferric-ellagate [25], higher H2O2 yield was achieved by Fe, N-CDs/RF under visible-light irradiation, likely due to Fe, N-CDs improving the electron dislocation degree of RF (confirmed by ESR) and effectively facilitating photo-induced charge separation, promoting the cycling of Fe3+/Fe2+. Simulated solar light (AM 1.5 G) was utilised to assess the impact of Fe, N-CDs/RF on H2O2 production (Fig. S13). Within one hour, the rates of generated H2O2 and SCC decreased, possibly due to the self-decomposition of Fe, N-CDs on H2O2 and the oxidation of the resin structure of the catalyst [26], reducing the production rate of H2O2. The SCC efficiency was approximately 0.68 % after 1 h of irradiation, suggesting that Fe, N-CDs/RF has a certain application value in artificial photocatalysis.

From the band edge position (Fig. 5b), it is evident that the ECB of RF was +0.38 eV vs RHE (0.21 eV vs RHE) [24], which was more negative than the reduction potential of O2/ H2O2 (+ 0.69 eV vs RHE) but insufficient to reduce O2 to ⋅O2 - (-0.33 eV vs RHE). Therefore, H2O2 was generated through 2-e- reduction in the RF system. Upon introducing Fe, N-CDs, the conduction band (CB) position shifted to +0.08 eV vs RHE, rendering the CB more negative and facilitating an increased number of reductive photo-generated electrons [25], thereby promoting H2O2 production. The EVB of RF was 2.30 eV vs RHE, while •OH generation potentials were 1.99 eV vs RHE (OH- /•OH) and 2.34 eV vs RHE (H2O/•OH), respectively. The •OH radical primarily converted OH- into •OH via light-induced generated h+ on RF surface [64]. After incorporating Fe, N-CDs, the ECB moved to 1.74 eV vs RHE. It cannot convert OH- into •OH within this system but rather facilitates •OH formation through catalytic decomposition of H2O2 by Fe, N-CDs. Since both VB positions exceeded the oxidation potential required for H2O (H2O/O2 +1.23 eV vs RHE), additional O2 addition during experimentation minimally impacted the overall degradation reaction (Fig. S14).

To gain a deeper understanding of the photocatalytic production of •OH from H2O2, we quantified both •OH and residual H2O2 generated during the process (Fig. 8b). The concentration of residual H2O2 in the solution directly correlated with the amount of •OH generated, indicating an excess production of H2O2 compared to its decomposition. The enhanced H2O2 generation confirmed that Fe, N-CDs modification achieved a more negative EVB, facilitating O2 reduction to H2O2. At a Fe, N-CDs content of 0.3 %, the maximum generation of •OH was observed, correlating with CQ degradation (Fig. S15). Subsequently, there was a decline in •OH concentration. Analysis revealed that part of •OH originated from h+ while another part came from H2O2 decomposition. However, increased Fe, N-CDs narrowed the bandgap (Eg) and reduced the ECB, resulting in insufficient h+ for oxidising H2O2 into OH- and subsequently reducing •OH concentration. At this stage, the band gap of Fe, N-CDs played a crucial role in H2O2 decomposition. By increasing the Fe, N-CDs content to 5.0 %, •OH concentration significantly improved to 0.16 μmol, indicating that Fe, N-CDs exhibited catalytic activity in decomposing H2O2 into •OH (Fig. S16). While higher amounts of Fe, N-CDs enhanced the efficiency activating H2O2 towards •OH formation, excessive Fe, N-CDs caused aggregation and occupation at sites responsible for H2O2 generating, thereby diminishing photo-generated electrons (consistent with photocurrent) and subsequently reduced overall H2O2 production. Simultaneously, excessive •OH may degrade the polymer catalyst itself. Thus, striking a balance between these two steps (production and decomposition H2O2) is crucial in the photocatalytic self-Fenton system initiated by Fe, N-CDs/RF.

To further probe the photoelectric separation characteristics of Fe, N-CDs/RF, steady-state photoluminescence (PL), electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and transient photocurrent response experiments were conducted. PL testing revealed a distinct peak at approximately 610 nm, indicating carrier composites ( Fig. 9a). Loading Fe, N-CDs onto the RF surface weaken luminescence intensity in Fe, N-CDs/RF-0.3 % suggesting enhanced charge separation ability.

It can be seen from the EIS (Fig. 9b) that Fe, N-CDs/RF has a smaller radius compared to pure RF, indicating a faster light-induced charge transfer rate and lower charge transfer impedance [62]. The lifetime measured by the time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) technique showed that the average fluorescence lifetime of Fe, N-CDs/RF-0.3 % (2.95 ns) was longer than that of pure RF (2.34 ns) (Fig. 9c). In addition, Fe, N-CDs/RF-0.3 % exhibited larger values for positive attenuation components τ1 and τ2, with τ2 proportion (59.17 %) higher than that of pure RF (45.73 %) (refer to Table S4 for supporting information). These results demonstrate that the introduction of Fe, N-CDs increases the non-radiative carrier recombination process within RF and improves carrier mobility [20], effectively promoting carriers separation at the interface.

To understand the photo-induced electron-hole separation behavior of Fe, N-CDs/RF-0.3 %, the photocurrent response of the material was measured (Fig. 9d). Under visible-light irradiation, the photocurrent density of Fe, N-CDs/RF was approximately 1.3 μA cm−2, higher than that of RF (0.45 μA cm−2). This improved photocurrent verified that efficient hole-electron separation and transfer were achieved by Fe, N-CDs modification [28]. Additionally, a transient peak of the current in the sample appeared when the lamp was turned on, possibly due to the sharp recombination of electrons and holes accumulated in the switching moment [24].

To investigate the role of active substances in Fe, N-CDs/RF-0.3 % system in CQ degradation, experiments were conducted using scavengers such as IPA, PBQ, and EDTA-2Na for •OH, •O2 −, and h+, respectively (Fig. S17). As shown in Fig. 10a, adding IPA reduced the CQ degradation rate to 24.9 %, indicating the dominance of •OH species in the system. Similarly, in the presence of PBQ, the degradation rate of CQ decreased to 58.8 %, suggesting the involvement of •O2 − in the CQ degradation process. The addition of EDTA-2Na slightly reduced CQ degradation by Fe, N-CDs/RF-0.3 %, implying that light-induced h+ had a minimal contribution to the oxidation of CQ. Both •OH and •O2 − were crucial in CQ removal. Thus, in the Fe, N-CDs/RF photo-Fenton system, the contribution order of active species to CQ degradation was: •OH> •O2 − >h+. It has been reported that the synergistic effect of •OH and h+ can aid in the ring-opening reaction of organic pollutants [62]. However, this catalyst only generated •OH, potentially limiting its CQ degradation rate.

EPR testing confirmed the generation of •OH, •O2 −, and 1O2. The characteristic •OH peak (1:2:2:1) observed in Fig. 10b indicated the presence of •OH. Analysis of the valence band (1.74 eV vs RHE) of Fe, N-CDs/RF suggested that photo-generated h+ were unable to convert OH- into •OH (1.99 eV vs RHE), which was generated through H2O2 cracking by Fe (II) in Fe, N-CDs, indicating efficient capability for decomposing in situ generated H2O2. Over time, there was a significant increase in the formation of both •O2 − and •OH (Fig. 10c), while a small amount of 1O2 was also detected (Fig. 10d).

To theoretically elucidate the underlying factors governing the separation of photo-generated carriers in Fe, N-CDs/RF, the density functional theory (DFT) calculations using the commercial simulation package VASP were conducted. Initially, the structures of RF and Fe, N-CDs/RF were optimised, followed by the calculation of charge density (Fig. S18) and density of states (DOS) ( Fig. 11a-b). Upon combing Fe, N-CDs with RF, electron enrichment near the Fe-O bond was observed, indicating increased electron delocalization [65]. The DOS (with Fermi energy set to 0 eV) revealed an increased DOS near the Fermi level after loading Fe, N-CDs. This observation was reinforced by the overlapping peaks between Fe-d and O-p orbitals, indicating that Fe, N-CDs can generate additional electrons, facilitating catalytic reactions, consistent with the photocurrent response. To validate electron transfer pathways between Fe, N-CDs sites and RF, charge density difference analysis along with Bader charge analysis were carried out (Fig. 11c-d). It demonstrated that RF transferred electrons (0.2275 e-) to Fe, N-CDs, enhancing electron-hole separation in RF and promoting the transformation cycle from Fe3+ to Fe2+. Activation of H2O2 into •OH is a crucial step in the Fenton reaction system. To further investigate the impact of Fe, N-CDs on H2O2 activation, the adsorption energy of Fe, N-CD/RF for H2O2 was calculated (Fig. 11e). Results revealed that the H2O2 had significantly lower adsorption energy on Fe, N-CDs (-5.45 eV) compared to RF (-0.92 eV), indicating favorable adsorption on Fe, N-CDs for rapid electrons transfer. The optimised structure showed H2O2 undergoing direct decomposition into *OH and subsequently adsorbed onto the Fe atom (Fe-OH) [44], similar to Fe-based metal enzymes producing •OH through O-O homolysis [64], thereby significantly improving H2O2 utilisation efficiency.

Based on the aforementioned results, we propose a cascade photocatalysis-self-Fenton process and mechanism for CQ removal using Fe, N-CDs/RF. XPS analysis confirmed the presence of both Fe2+ and Fe3+ ions in Fe, N-CDs. Under visible-light irradiation, RF generated electrons and holes (Eq.1). Some photo-generated electrons reduced O2 to H2O2 (Eq.2), while others were promptly captured by Fe, N-CDs on the RF surface, facilitating rapid electron transfer to Fe3+ [44], thereby reducing it to Fe2+ (Eq.3). Fe, N-CDs exhibited outstanding reaction activity due to their strong adsorption ability towards H2O2 (Eq. 4), Fe2+-CDs can in situ decompose the H2O2 generated by RF into •OH (Eq.5, Eq.6), actively participating in the degradation reaction of CQ. According to the band structure analysis (Fig. 5b), it can be inferred that O2 reduction (Eq. 2) occurs through a 2-e- reduction process (Eq.7, Eq. 8) [9]. Consequently, •O2 - was also observed (confirmed via EPR in Fig. 9c), which can react with h+ to produce 1O2 [66].
(1) RF + hν→RF (e-)+ RF (h+)
(2) RF (2e-) +2H++O2→H2O2 +RF
(3) OH-Fe3+, N-CDs+ RF (e-)→RF+ OH-Fe2+, N-CDs(e)
(4) 2Fe2+, N-CDs + H2O2→2OH-Fe2+ N-CDs
(5) 2OH-Fe2+, N-CDs→•OH+ 2Fe3+, N-CDs+OH-
(6) 2OH-Fe2+, N-CDs→2•OH+ 2Fe2+, N-CDs+H+
(7) O2+2e-+2H+→•O2 - (+0.68 V vs NHE)
(8) •O2 -+e-+2H+→H2O2 (+1.44 V vs NHE)

Compared to RF, Fe, N-CDs/RF exhibited enhanced degradation efficiency towards CQ due to several factors: (1) Fe, N-CDs modification altering RF surface charge from negative to positive and transforming its hydrophobicity into hydrophilicity, promoting photocatalytic reactions; (2) Successful RF and Fe, N-CDs coupling narrowing the band gap (from 1.92 eV to 1.66 eV), enhancing visible-light utilisation and photocatalytic activity; (3) Fe, N-CDs acting as electron acceptors, suppressing electrons-holes recombination in RF and augmenting H2O2 production. Additionally, nano-enzyme performance and a strong adsorption capacity for H2O2 facilitated •OH generation. Compared to RF resin, Fe, N-CDs/RF exhibited a 3.2-fold increase in the degradation rate constant towards CQ. Furthermore, compared with other catalytic degradation systems, the photo-Fenton system based on Fe, N-CDs/RF offers advantages such as the elimination of the need for additional H2O2 introduction, easy access to raw materials, environmental friendliness, responsiveness to visible-light irradiation and suitability for use within a neutral pH range.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible CQ degradation pathway and toxicity assessment
ce:section.ce:para: In order to better understand the degradation pathways of CQ, HPLC-MS was used to detect the substances produced at the end of the CQ degradation by Fe, N-CDs/RF-0.3 % (Table S5 and Fig. S19). Based on the results of the assay, a possible degradation pathway of CQ was proposed, as shown in Fig. 12. Two products, D2 and D3, were detected through the substituent cleavage reaction of the parent D1 side chain, which was attributed to the fact that the nitrogen atoms in the structure of CQ are prone to lose electrons and are more susceptible to electronophilic substances, such as •OH, •O2 -, and 1O2 [67], which attacked the side chain of CQ through a series of consecutive deamination and decarbonylation processes, becoming the main degradation pathway. This process results in the formation of D2 and D3. The C-N, C-C bond of pyridine ring and benzene ring in the presence of reactive oxygen species can be attacked and formed D4 and D5. D3 also underwent a series of deamination and oxidation reactions to form the products D6, D7, D8, D9, and D10, which was consistent with previous studies [3,5]. Finally, these small molecular weight intermediates mineralized to H2O, CO2, NO3 - etc., as confirmed by the TOC results, which showed that the rate of mineralization reached 54.6 %.

In addition, the toxicity of CQ and metabolites were evaluated by Toxicity Estimation Software (T.E.S.T.). As shown in Fig. 12a-c, the toxicity of CQ and degradation products decreased considerably with the progress of Fenton oxidation and were essentially non-harmful except for D3. Fig. 12d showed that the photo-Fenton process developed in Fe, N-CDs/RF reduced the bioaccumulation factors of most of the intermediates except product D4. This indicated that the system is an environmentally friendly and efficient system ( Fig. 13).

The environmental toxicity of CQ and its degradation intermediates was assessed by seed germination experiments. Rye seeds, sudangrass seeds and tall fescue seeds were selected as model plants. As shown in Fig. 14, all seeds germinated after 3 days of incubation. The root and stem lengths of the three grasses were about 5–14 cm, 2–7 cm and 4–7 cm, respectively. By comparing the seeds in different aqueous solutions, it was found that ryegrass was more tolerant to CQ, while the growth of sudangrass and tall fescue was limited in CQ solution, especially for sudangrass. After 60 min and 120 min of photodegradation, sudan grass seeds showed the best growth trend compared to those cultured in deionized water, which may be due to the better sensitivity of sudan grass to environmental toxicity. Ryegrass and tall fescue grew well in the CQ degradation solution, suggesting that nitrate and carbonate in the CQ metabolites contributed to seed growth [68]. This indicates that the environmental toxicity of CQ degradation intermediates is low.

To achieve the in situ production and efficient utilisation of H2O2 and •OH, a catalyst combing nanozyme and photocatalytic was developed by loading Fe, N-CDs onto the surface of RF. Fe, N-CDs functioned as activators for H2O2 and accelerated the reduction reaction of O2 under visible-light, thus facilitating H2O2 generation. Furthermore, Fe, N-CDs possessed carbon dots features and catalase-like activity. The electrons generated by RF were rapidly transferred to Fe, N-CDs, which broadening the light absorption range and improving the separation efficiency. Fe, N-CDs also demonstrated strong adsorption capacity for H2O2, efficiently catalysing it to •OH in situ, enhancing the photocatalytic degradation rate of CQ by 3.2 times compared to RF. However, there is still room for improvement in catalytic performance. At the same time, the Fe, N-CDs/RF light-Fenton system was highly resistant to anionic interference, and there was no need to adjust the pH of the solution and add additional H2O2 during the test. The degraded biotoxicity was significantly reduced in the solution compared to the original CQ. Moreover, the easy separation properties, high catalytic activity in different water qualities exhibited by Fe, N-CDs/RF material facilitate practical application. Therefore, this study presents a nanozyme that can be used in the photocatalysis-self-Fenton system to trigger the Fenton reaction, inspiring the rational design and preparation of highly stable and active photocatalytic-self-Fenton materials for treating practical wastewater with CQ and other pollutions.

Guijing Chen: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Resources, Data curation. Jie Yu: Supervision. Mingze An: Funding acquisition. Ting Lei: Methodology, Investigation. Qingqing Qin: Investigation. Shuhao Qin: Project administration, Funding acquisition. Min He: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Yuanyuan Yang: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation.
